message.content:
## Web Searcher: rate-aware-TTS/fine-tune-vs-timestretch

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Your angle: **rate-aware-TTS/fine-tune-vs-timestretch** — Covers angle 3: the critical question of measurable comprehensibility gain from native fast generation/fine-tuning versus post-hoc time-compression.
Search query: `speaking rate controllable neural TTS fast speech generation fine-tuning vs time-scale modification intelligibility gain clear speech Lombard corpus`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Web search results for query: "speaking rate controllable neural TTS fast speech generation fine-tuning vs time-scale modification intelligibility gain clear speech Lombard corpus"

Links: [{"title":"Speaking-Rate-Controllable HiFi-GAN Using Feature Interpolation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.10561"},{"title":"STSM-FiLM: A FiLM-Conditioned Neural Architecture for Time-Scale Modification of Speech","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.02672"},{"title":"FastSpeech: New text-to-speech model improves on speed, accuracy, and controllability - Microsoft Research","url":"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/fastspeech-new-text-to-speech-model-improves-on-speed-accuracy-and-controllability/"},{"title":"Automatic signal adjustment based on intelligibility","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7653543"},{"title":"Word-Level Emotional Expression Control in Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech Synthesis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.24629"},{"title":"Total-Duration-Aware Duration Modeling for Text-to-Speech Systems","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.04281"},{"title":"Non-Attentive Tacotron: Robust and Controllable Neural TTS Synthesis   Including Unsupervised Duration Modeling","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.04301"},{"title":"Seed-TTS: A Family of High-Quality Versatile Speech Generation Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2406.02430v1"}]

I found several relevant research papers and resources on this topic. Here are the key findings from the web search:

**Speaking Rate Control in Neural TTS:**

FastSpeech can adjust voice speed through a length regulator, varying speed from 0.5x to 1.5x without loss of voice quality. Instruction fine-tuning (IFT) enables neural TTS models to flexibly control each aspect of generated speech such as expressiveness, speaking rate, style, and emotion.

**Time-Scale Modification vs. Neural Approaches:**

The most prevailing method for speaking rate control is time-scale modification (TSM) algorithms based on waveform warping. However, recent neural time-scale modification methods have emerged as alternatives to classical signal processing, leveraging deep learning to preserve perceptual smoothness, temporal coherence, and intelligibility under varying speed changes.

**Intelligibility Considerations:**

Real-time TSM has the potential to improve speech intelligibility in noisy environments, offering practical benefits for listeners with hearing difficulties or in challenging acoustic conditions. Speaking rate changes speech intelligibility and accessibility, with users needing different speaking rates depending on the situation.

The search results included technical papers on FastSpeech, STSM-FiLM, and other neural TTS approaches, though I didn't find specific results mentioning "Lombard corpus" in these particular results.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "naturally produced fast speech vs time-compressed speech intelligibility comprehension difference"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech","url":"https://www.academia.edu/115224083/Review_of_research_on_the_intelligibility_and_comprehension_of_accelerated_speech"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282424493_Intelligibility_of_time-compressed_synthetic_speech_Compression_method_and_speaking_style"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222400427_Word_perception_in_fast_speech_Artificially_time-compressed_vs_naturally_produced_fast_speech"},{"title":"(PDF) Word perception in natural-fast and artificially time-compressed speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/46640230_Word_perception_in_natural-fast_and_artificially_time-compressed_speech"},{"title":"Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/293804518_Natural_fast_speech_is_perceived_as_faster_than_linearly_time-compressed_speech"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Max Planck Institute","url":"https://www.mpi.nl/publications/item66910/word-perception-fast-speech-artificially-time-compressed-vs-naturally"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings on the differences between naturally produced fast speech and time-compressed speech regarding intelligibility and comprehension:

## Processing Speed Differences

Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech.

## Comprehension Advantage

Linearly time-compressed speech has a temporal and a segmental processing advantage over naturally produced fast speech. Additionally, listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech.

## Speech Rate Considerations

The relationship between speech types varies depending on compression rates. Linearly compressed speech was found to be more intelligible and preferred over a nonlinearly compressed version of speech where fast speech prosodic patterns were mimicked at a high speaking rate (2.85 times). However, at ultra fast speaking rates (2.5 and 4.1 times) MACH1 improves comprehension and is preferable to linearly compressed speech but no advantage was found at the fast speech speaking rate (1.4 times).

## Intelligibility of Natural Fast Speech

Natural fast sentences were still intelligible despite natural acceleration. This suggests that while naturally produced fast speech remains understandable, it requires more cognitive processing compared to artificially time-compressed speech.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "clear speech hyperarticulation training corpus TTS synthesis intelligibility gain over plain speech time-compressed"

Links: [{"title":"Utterance Selection for Optimizing Intelligibility of TTS Voices Trained on","url":"https://www.cs.columbia.edu/speech/PaperFiles/2017/is_17_cooper.pdf"},{"title":"Covertly improving intelligibility with data-driven adaptations of speech timing","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2603.30032"},{"title":"Training Articulatory Inversion Models for Interspeaker Consistency","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.20529"},{"title":"LOMBARD SPEECH SYNTHESIS FOR ANY VOICE WITH CONTROLLABLE STYLE EMBEDDINGS","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.12966"},{"title":"Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in Text-To-Speech Synthesis using Speaking","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.05809"},{"title":"(PDF) Perception of Concatenative vs. Neural Text-To-Speech (TTS): Differences in Intelligibility in Noise and Language Attitudes","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/344893836_Perception_of_Concatenative_vs_Neural_Text-To-Speech_TTS_Differences_in_Intelligibility_in_Noise_and_Language_Attitudes"},{"title":"Improved Dysarthric Speech to Text Conversion via TTS Personalization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.06391"},{"title":"The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension under adverse listening conditions","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9177484/"},{"title":"Plain-to-clear speech video conversion for enhanced intelligibility","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10042924/"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what I found regarding clear speech, hyperarticulation, training corpora, TTS synthesis, and intelligibility gains:

## Clear Speech and Hyperarticulation in TTS

When produced by L1 speakers, foreigner-directed speech massively features a reduction of speech rate as well as an omnibus lengthening and hyperarticulation of vowels. While these strategies are typically evaluated subjectively as more intelligible by L2-participants, there is debate whether they in fact improve objective word comprehension.

## Intelligibility Gains from Modified Speech Styles

Research on specialized TTS approaches shows substantial intelligibility improvements. A Lombard-SSDRC TTS system shows significant relative improvement between 110% and 130% in speech-shaped noise, and 47% to 140% in competing-speaker noise against state-of-the-art TTS approaches.

## Corpus-Based Training

Studies have selected subsets of utterances based on different factors such as speaking rate, f0 and energy mean and standard deviation, and level of articulation. Additionally, Lombard TTS methods can enhance intelligibility under noise while preserving naturalness and speaker identity with fine-grained control over prosody.

## Clear Speech vs. Time-Compressed Speech

Listeners judge targeted speech-rate structure subjectively less intelligible than a general slowdown, although the latter actually increases word error rate. This suggests nuanced differences between clear speech strategies and simple time-compression approaches.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Lombard speech synthesis TTS intelligibility in noise neural fine-tuning improvement measurable"

Links: [{"title":"Whispered and Lombard Neural Speech Synthesis","url":"http://www.vertexdoc.com/doc/whispered-and-lombard-neural-speech-synthesis"},{"title":"Normal-to-Lombard adaptation of speech synthesis using long short-term memory recurrent neural networks - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639318303832"},{"title":"whispered and lombard neural speech synthesis - ar5iv - arXiv","url":"https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2101.05313"},{"title":"Whispered and Lombard Neural Speech Synthesis | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/350396142_Whispered_and_Lombard_Neural_Speech_Synthesis"},{"title":"Self-Adaptive Incremental Machine Speech Chain for Lombard TTS with High-Granularity ASR Feedback in Dynamic Noise Condition | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/371287320_Self-Adaptive_Incremental_Machine_Speech_Chain_for_Lombard_TTS_with_High-Granularity_ASR_Feedback_in_Dynamic_Noise_Condition"},{"title":"LOMBARD SPEECH SYNTHESIS FOR ANY VOICE WITH CONTROLLABLE STYLE EMBEDDINGS","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.12966"},{"title":"Normal-to-Lombard Adaptation of Speech Synthesis Using ...","url":"https://research.aalto.fi/files/33417584/ELEC_Bollepalli_Normal_to_lombard_Speech_Communication.pdf"},{"title":"WHISPERED AND LOMBARD NEURAL SPEECH SYNTHESIS","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.05313"},{"title":"Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in Text-To-Speech Synthesis using Speaking","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.05809"},{"title":"A Machine Speech Chain Approach for Dynamically Adaptive Lombard TTS in Static and Dynamic Noise Environments | IEEE Journals & Magazine | IEEE Xplore","url":"https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9851526/"}]

I've completed the web search with your query about Lombard speech synthesis, TTS intelligibility in noise, and neural fine-tuning improvements. Here's a summary of what the research shows:

## Key Findings on Lombard Speech Synthesis

**Intelligibility Improvements:**
Lombard speech improves intelligibility significantly over Normal TTS, with research demonstrating measurable gains when TTS systems are adapted to Lombard speaking style.

**Neural Fine-Tuning Approaches:**
DNN-based adaptation methods utilize auxiliary features (AF), learning hidden unit contribution (LHUC), and fine-tuning (FT), originally introduced for speaker adaptation in both speech synthesis and recognition. Phoneme durations were also adapted from normal to Lombard with the fine-tuning adaptation method.

**Measurable Performance Gains:**
Research has documented significant quantifiable improvements. Intelligibility enhancement as quantified by the Intelligibility in Bits (SIIB Gauss) measure shows that the proposed Lombard TTS system shows significant relative improvement between 110% and 130% in speech-shaped noise, and 47% to 140% in competing-speaker noise. Subjective evaluation shows Lombard TTS successfully increases speech intelligibility with relative improvement of 455% for speech-shaped noise and 104% for competing-speaker noise in median keyword correction rate.

**Recent Developments:**
Recent evaluations demonstrate that methods preserve naturalness and speaker identity, enhance intelligibility under noise, and provide fine-grained control over prosody.

The research consistently shows that neural fine-tuning approaches effectively adapt TTS systems to produce Lombard speech with measurable improvements in intelligibility across various noise conditions.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.query: ultra-fast speech screen reader power users 18-22 syllables per second comprehension training neural TTS native fast generation gain

content.content:
Web search results for query: "ultra-fast speech screen reader power users 18-22 syllables per second comprehension training neural TTS native fast generation gain"

Links: [{"title":"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech-blind vs.\" normally hearing\" persons | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228846770_Comprehension_of_ultra-fast_speech-blind_vs_normally_hearing_persons"},{"title":"Why Can Some Blind People Process Speech Far Faster Than Sighted Persons? | Scientific American","url":"https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/why-can-some-blind-people-process/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant research on ultra-fast speech comprehension by screen reader users and blind individuals:

## Key Findings on Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension

**Speech Speed Capabilities:**
Vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second, far exceeding normal sighted listeners' upper limit of approximately 8 syllables per second. Some blind people can comprehend speech sped up to 25 syllables per second.

**Screen Reader Applications:**
This exceptional skill allows for the processing of large amounts of written materials using screen-reading text-to-speech devices and may help cope with demands of college or university education.

**Training and Brain Plasticity:**
Acquisition of ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech perception skills induces de novo central-visual hemodynamic activation in late-blind participants. In a training study, four of six participants showed considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception through specific training measures over approximately 6 months.

**Neural Mechanisms:**
Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar regions.

The research demonstrates that ultra-fast speech comprehension is a trainable skill in screen reader users, with neural reorganization occurring in response to training.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Study Findings on Time-Compressed vs. Natural Fast Speech

## Key Finding

The research demonstrates that recognition of time-compressed (TC) speech **significantly overestimates** comprehension of naturally produced fast speech, particularly for older adults.

## Quantified Differences

The study found consistent performance gaps across conditions:

- **In quiet conditions**: Listeners performed substantially better with TC speech than natural fast speech, especially for low-context sentences
- **In noise**: The disparity widened, with older listeners showing significantly worse performance for natural fast speech compared to TC speech (p = 0.005)
- **Correlation analysis**: Nearly all individual data points fell above the prediction line, indicating "recognition of TC materials was consistently higher than recognition of natural-fast speech materials"

## Why the Difference?

Natural fast speech contains acoustic distortions absent in TC speech:

> "segmental deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates...associated with co-articulation"

TC algorithms preserve temporal structure and spectral information uniformly, whereas naturally fast speech exhibits selective segmental changes, deletions (particularly schwa deletion), and articulation undershoot.

## Practical Implication

The researchers conclude that laboratory testing using TC speech may substantially underestimate real-world listening difficulties, especially for aging populations who experience the greatest comprehension challenges with naturally fast speech.

content.text:
I now have comprehensive findings covering the critical question from multiple angles. The PMC6377055 study is the strongest direct evidence, and the Speech Communication paper on natural-fast vs. time-compressed perception is the canonical citation. Let me compile the top results.

The key insight for the original question: the research finding actually runs counter to naive intuition. Time-compressed (TSM) speech is consistently MORE intelligible and better-comprehended than naturally produced fast speech at matched rates, because natural fast speech suffers segmental deletions and articulation undershoot. This means native fast generation by a TTS doesn't automatically beat time-compression unless it specifically targets clear-speech/Lombard articulation. The measurable gains from fine-tuning come from clear/Lombard styles (110-455% intelligibility-in-noise gains), not from "fast" generation per se.

results.title: Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners (PMC)
results.snippet: Direct, quantified answer to the core question and a crucial counterintuitive result: artificially time-compressed (TSM) speech is recognized CONSISTENTLY BETTER than naturally produced fast speech at matched rates (gap widens in noise, p=0.005 for older listeners). Reason: natural fast speech suffers segmental deletions, stop/affricate distortions, schwa deletion and articulation undershoot, whereas TSM uniformly preserves spectral/temporal structure. Implication for the pipeline: simply generating 'fast' speech does NOT beat time-compressing a normal voice unless the model specifically targets clear articulation. This sets the bar a native-fast fine-tune must clear.

results.title: Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech (Speech Communication, Janse)
results.snippet: Canonical peer-reviewed study establishing that even when naturally produced fast speech is fully intelligible, its less-careful articulation plus changed timing pattern SLOWS DOWN word processing relative to linear time-compression. Linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental processing advantage, and listeners prefer it. Core evidence that post-hoc TSM is a strong baseline; a native-fast neural TTS must add clear-speech articulation to win.

results.title: Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in Text-To-Speech Synthesis using Speaking Style Conversion / Lombard-SSDRC (arXiv)
results.snippet: Quantifies the measurable gain from fine-tuning/conditioning a neural TTS toward a clear/Lombard speaking style rather than just speeding it up. Lombard-SSDRC TTS yields 110-130% relative intelligibility improvement (SIIB) in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise vs. SOTA TTS; subjective keyword-correct gains up to 455%. Shows WHERE the real comprehensibility payoff of training lives (clear/Lombard articulation), distinct from raw speaking-rate increase. Directly informs the 'is fine-tuning worth it' decision.

results.title: FastSpeech: TTS model with length-regulator speaking-rate control (Microsoft Research)
results.snippet: Foundational reference for NATIVE rate-controllable neural TTS: FastSpeech's length regulator scales the phoneme-duration predictor to vary speed 0.5x-1.5x without quality loss, and exposes per-phoneme duration so vowels vs. consonants can be compressed differently (content-aware variable-rate). This is the mechanism by which a neural model can natively generate fast speech with controllable rhythm rather than post-hoc TSM. Same non-attentive duration-modeling family underlies Kokoro/Piper, so the knobs are locally available.

results.title: Lombard Speech Synthesis for Any Voice with Controllable Style Embeddings (arXiv)
results.snippet: State-of-the-art on conditioning/fine-tuning neural TTS to produce clear/Lombard speech with controllable style embeddings while preserving naturalness and speaker identity, with fine-grained prosody control. Most directly applicable recipe for the user's 'fine-tune an open model' effort tier: shows you can add a clear-speech style axis to an existing voice rather than training from scratch, and reports intelligibility-in-noise gains. Speaks to whether the fine-tune clears a significant-gain bar.

results.title: Covertly improving intelligibility with data-driven adaptations of speech timing (arXiv)
results.snippet: Nuanced cautionary evidence on rate/timing manipulation: listeners JUDGE targeted speech-rate restructuring as less intelligible than a general slowdown, yet the general slowdown actually raises word error rate. Highlights that subjective preference and objective intelligibility can diverge for timing/rate edits, which matters for designing the A/B/MUSHRA evaluation and for choosing per-phoneme variable-rate compression strategy over uniform changes. Supports the 'measure before committing' requirement.

content.text:
I've completed the research on the rate-aware-TTS / fine-tune-vs-timestretch angle and returned 6 ranked results via StructuredOutput.

Key takeaway for the orchestrator's synthesis: the literature delivers a counterintuitive but decision-critical answer. Linearly time-compressed (TSM) speech is consistently MORE intelligible and faster to process than naturally produced fast speech at matched rates (PMC6377055; Janse, Speech Communication 2003), because natural fast speech suffers segmental deletions and articulation undershoot that TSM avoids. So merely making a neural TTS "generate fast" buys little or nothing over time-compressing a normal voice. The measurable gain from fine-tuning comes specifically from clear-speech / Lombard articulation conditioning (110-455% intelligibility-in-noise improvements in the Lombard-SSDRC and controllable-style-embedding work), and from FastSpeech-style per-phoneme duration control enabling content-aware variable-rate compression rather than uniform speedup. This means the "fine-tune is worth it" bar is cleared only if the fine-tune targets clear/Lombard articulation, not raw rate.
